title: On-Demand vs Reserved/Savings Plans vs Spot
description:
## Pricing a steady baseline (and a batch job)

A workload runs **24/7 at a steady, predictable baseline** for the foreseeable future and must always be available. Separately, the team also runs a fault-tolerant, checkpointed batch-processing job that can be paused and resumed at any time. Which purchasing strategy is most cost-effective for the steady 24/7 baseline?

options.text: Cover the steady 24/7 baseline with a commitment-based discount (Reserved Instances or a Savings Plan), trading a 1- or 3-year usage commitment for a substantial discount versus On-Demand, since the baseline is predictable and always running. (The interruptible, fault-tolerant batch job is the natural fit for Spot capacity, which is cheapest but can be reclaimed with little notice.)
Run the steady baseline entirely on Spot instances; because the baseline is predictable, Spot reclamation will never actually happen, making it the cheapest always-on option.

Keep the steady baseline on pure On-Demand pricing; On-Demand is cheaper than Reserved for anything that runs continuously because you avoid an upfront commitment, and reservations only pay off for spiky traffic.
Put the steady baseline on Spot and the fault-tolerant batch job on a 3-year Reserved Instance, since long-running reservations should be reserved for jobs that can tolerate interruption.

explanation: A **steady, predictable, always-on** baseline is the canonical case for a **commitment-based discount** — Reserved Instances or a Savings Plan — where you commit to a level of usage for 1 or 3 years in exchange for a large discount off On-Demand. Because the workload runs continuously and predictably, you will actually consume the committed capacity, so the discount is realized: that is (a). **Spot (b/d)** offers the deepest discount but the provider can reclaim the capacity with little notice; predictability of *your* load does not stop the provider from reclaiming Spot capacity, so an always-available baseline must never depend on Spot — (b)'s premise is false. Spot is instead ideal for **interruptible, fault-tolerant, checkpointed** work like the batch job — which is why (d) is backwards (it puts the must-stay-up baseline on Spot and wastes a 3-year reservation on the interruptible job). **Pure On-Demand (c)** is the right tool for short-lived or unpredictable spikes, but for steady 24/7 usage it is more expensive than a reservation/Savings Plan, so (c)'s claim that On-Demand beats Reserved for continuous workloads is exactly wrong. The cost-optimal mix: reservation/Savings Plan for the predictable baseline, On-Demand for unpredictable bursts, and Spot for fault-tolerant batch.

hints.title: Match commitment to predictability
hints.body: Steady, always-on usage justifies a 1- or 3-year commitment (Reserved/Savings Plan) because you'll use the capacity you committed to and earn the discount.

hints.title: Spot's catch
hints.body: Spot is cheapest but interruptible at short notice regardless of how predictable your own demand is — only safe for fault-tolerant/checkpointed/batch work, never a must-stay-up baseline.
